title: Slovakia Scholarships for Talented International Students 2026/27: Study in Europe and Receive Up to €15,000
summary: Applications are available for the Slovakia Scholarships for Talented Students from All Over the World for the 2026/2027 academic year , an international funding opportunity created to attract talented students to selected public and state higher education institutions in the Slovak Republic. The scholarship supports eligible international students who wish to pursue full-time bachelor’s, combined bachelor’s and master’s, or master’s degree studies in Slovakia. Successful applicants may receive €500 per month for ten months of each academic year , with support lasting up to three years for bachelor’s or combined programmes and up to two years for master’s programmes. This means that eligible bachelor’s and combined-degree students may receive up to €15,000 , while master’s students may receive up to €10,000 over two academic years. The programme is open to applicants from around the world, including certain Slovaks living abroad, provided they meet the academic, residency, admission and standardised-test requirements. The official application banner states that applications are open and must be submitted by 31 July 2026 at 23:59 CEST . Applicants should nevertheless check the active application portal carefully, because a later section of the published information appears to contain older wording stating that registration had not yet opened. About the Slovakia Scholarships for Talented International Students The scholarship programme is part of Slovakia’s effort to attract talented international students and strengthen higher education in fields connected to the country’s development priorities. It is intended for students who plan to begin or continue eligible higher education studies in Slovakia during the 2026/2027 academic year . The programme supports study at selected public or state Slovak higher education institutions. Applicants must choose an eligible full-time programme connected to the Smart Specialisation Strategy of the Slovak Republic, SK RIS3 2021+ . This means that not every university, faculty or degree programme in Slovakia will automatically qualify. Applicants must confirm that both their chosen institution and study programme appear on the official list of supported programmes before submitting their scholarship application. The scholarship is designed to support talented students while encouraging them to pursue studies in areas considered important for Slovakia’s academic, economic, scientific and technological development. Scholarship Value and Financial Support The scholarship provides a monthly payment of: €500 per month Paid for ten months of each academic year Normally covering the period from September to June The academic year in Slovakia begins on 1 September and ends on 31 August of the following year, but scholarship payments are provided for ten eligible months rather than the full twelve-month period. Funding for Bachelor’s Students Students enrolled in an eligible full-time bachelor’s programme may receive: €5,000 per academic year Up to three academic years of support A maximum total value of €15,000 Funding for Combined Bachelor’s and Master’s Students Students enrolled in a supported combined bachelor’s and master’s programme may receive: €5,000 per academic year Support for the first three academic years A maximum total value of €15,000 The scholarship does not necessarily fund the entire duration of a longer combined programme. The published information specifies support for the first three continuing years. Funding for Master’s Students Students enrolled in an eligible full-time master’s programme may receive: €5,000 per academic year Up to two academic years of support A maximum total value of €10,000 Although the programme is promoted as offering scholarships of up to €15,000, the maximum available to a two-year master’s student is normally €10,000 under the stated monthly payment structure. Number of Scholarships Available The programme plans to award up to 225 scholarships across bachelor’s, combined bachelor’s and master’s study levels. Bachelor’s and Combined Degree Scholarships Up to 170 scholarships may be awarded to students entering bachelor’s or combined bachelor’s and master’s programmes. These places are divided as follows: 110 scholarships for students attending public higher education institutions outside the Bratislava region 60 scholarships for students attending public higher education institutions in the Bratislava region Master’s Scholarships Up to 55 scholarships may be awarded to master’s students. These places are divided as follows: 35 scholarships for students attending public higher education institutions outside the Bratislava region 20 scholarships for students attending public higher education institutions in the Bratislava region The Ministry may adjust the number of awards where necessary. During the evaluation process, applicants may also be organised into geographical groups covering Europe; the United States, Canada, Australia, New Zealand and Oceania; South America; Central America and Africa; and Asia. Eligible Levels of Study The scholarship applies only to the following study levels: Full-time bachelor’s degree programmes Full-time combined bachelor’s and master’s programmes Full-time master’s degree programmes The scholarship does not support doctoral or PhD studies. All supported programmes must be: Full-time Conducted in person Delivered at an eligible public or state Slovak higher education institution Included within the selected areas of Slovakia’s Smart Specialisation Strategy Part-time, external or distance-learning programmes do not qualify. Eligibility for Bachelor’s Applicants Applicants seeking funding for a bachelor’s or combined bachelor’s and master’s programme must generally meet the following conditions: They must be applying from abroad. They must have completed their secondary education outside Slovakia. They must plan to begin an eligible bachelor’s or combined programme in Slovakia during the 2026/2027 academic year. They must not have previously studied at a higher education institution in Slovakia, except through an eligible academic mobility arrangement. They must not have previously attended secondary school in Slovakia, subject to the stated exception for qualifying Slovaks living abroad. They must apply to or receive admission from a supported public Slovak higher education institution. At the time of submitting the scholarship application, it may be sufficient for the applicant to have already applied to the university. However, the student must eventually be admitted, officially enrolled and begin the eligible programme before scholarship payments can be made. Eligibility for Master’s Applicants Master’s scholarships are available to several categories of applicants. International Graduates Applying from Abroad Applicants may qualify if they: Completed their bachelor’s degree outside Slovakia Are entering a master’s programme in Slovakia for the first time Apply to an eligible full-time master’s programme at a supported institution Students Progressing From a Slovak Bachelor’s Degree Applicants who completed a bachelor’s degree at a Slovak higher education institution may also qualify if they: Progress directly and continuously to the master’s level Have not previously received support under the relevant Recovery and Resilience Plan scholarship scheme Previous Scholarship Recipients Students who previously received support under the earlier Recovery and Resilience Plan scholarship scheme at bachelor’s level may qualify if they: Successfully complete their bachelor’s degree Progress continuously to an eligible master’s programme For master’s applicants, previous study at a Slovak higher education institution does not automatically disqualify the applicant. Citizenship and Residence Requirements Applicants must normally hold citizenship of a country other than Slovakia. Citizenship must be demonstrated through an acceptable identity document, generally: A valid passport A national identity card for eligible citizens of the European Union, European Economic Area or Switzerland Dual citizens who hold both Slovak citizenship and another citizenship may apply under the stated rules. Slovaks living abroad may also qualify, but they must submit the appropriate certificate issued under Slovak legislation concerning Slovaks living abroad. Applicants must also demonstrate that they have not held permanent residence in Slovakia at any time from 1 January 2021 until the date of submitting the scholarship application . This condition is normally confirmed through a hand-signed solemn declaration. Temporary residence in Slovakia may not automatically make an applicant ineligible. The published announcement specifically notes that some holders of temporary residence permits may still qualify, depending on the programme’s conditions and exceptions. Admission to a Slovak University The scholarship application and university admission process are separate. Receiving the scholarship does not automatically mean that the applicant has been admitted to a university. Similarly, applying to a university does not automatically produce a scholarship application. Applicants must therefore complete two distinct processes: Apply directly to an eligible Slovak higher education institution for admission. Submit a separate online application for the scholarship. Applicants should communicate with their preferred university as early as possible to confirm: Whether the programme is supported Admission requirements Required academic documents Language requirements Tuition fees Recognition of previous qualifications Enrolment dates Programme-specific deadlines The official scholarship information strongly advises applicants to contact their selected institution early to avoid delays or misunderstandings. Standardised Test Requirement The selection process is based on the applicant’s results in an approved standardised test. Applicants must submit results from either: The SAT, administered by the College Board; or The SCIO Mathematics and General Academic Prerequisites tests The standardised-test result is described as the only criterion used to determine the ranking of eligible applicants. Documents such as the CV and motivation letter are still mandatory, but applicants who do not meet the required test threshold will not proceed in the selection process. Minimum SAT Score Applicants submitting SAT results must achieve: A minimum total SAT score of 1,100 points Equivalent to approximately the 63rd percentile under the programme’s stated system SAT results from tests taken between 1 January 2021 and the application deadline may be accepted. The announcement identified 6 June 2026 as the latest relevant SAT date for the current cycle. Applicants applying close to the July deadline would therefore generally need to have already taken the examination and obtained an eligible result. Minimum SCIO Score Applicants using SCIO results must take both: Mathematics, known as MAT General Academic Prerequisites, known as VŠP The applicant must achieve an average percentile of at least 63 across the two tests. The application system calculates the average using the percentile achieved in each examination. Applicants must submit an official SCIO certificate. One SCIO test alone is not sufficient because both Mathematics and General Academic Prerequisites are required. Selection Process After the application deadline, the Ministry of Education, Research, Development and Youth of the Slovak Republic or the relevant higher education institutions will verify submitted applications. The assessment may include confirmation of: Citizenship Residence history Educational background University application or admission Eligibility of the selected programme Full-time study status Standardised-test score Completeness and authenticity of documents Compliance with all declarations and requirements Eligible applicants will be ranked according to their accepted SAT or SCIO results. Applicants below the minimum threshold will not be considered. Meeting the minimum score does not guarantee selection because the number of scholarships is limited and the applicant’s ranking among other eligible candidates will also matter. Preliminary and final lists of successful applicants will be prepared after verification and enrolment procedures. Scholarship payments begin only after the student has: Been officially admitted Enrolled at the selected institution Commenced the eligible study programme Completed the required administrative process Where administrative delays occur, eligible payments may be made retroactively for the months during which the student qualified. Documents Required for the Application Applicants should prepare the following documents. 1. Identity Document Applicants must provide a certified copy of a valid passport or another accepted identity document. 2. Declaration of Permanent Residence A hand-signed solemn declaration must confirm that the applicant has not held permanent residence in Slovakia between 1 January 2021 and the application deadline. 3. Certificate for Slovaks Living Abroad This is required only for applicants applying under the special eligibility provisions for Slovaks living abroad. 4. Legal Guardian’s Consent Applicants under 18 must submit a hand-signed consent form from their legal guardian concerning the processing of personal data. 5. SAT or SCIO Certificate Applicants must provide proof of: An SAT score of at least 1,100; or An average SCIO percentile of at least 63 across Mathematics and General Academic Prerequisites 6. University Acceptance Letter Applicants should upload an acceptance letter from a Slovak higher education institution where available. The published rules indicate that an applicant may still apply for the scholarship after applying to the university but before receiving final admission. Nevertheless, official admission and enrolment will be required before the scholarship can be awarded and paid. 7. Curriculum Vitae A structured CV in English is mandatory. The official template should be used where required by the application portal. 8. Motivation Letter Applicants must submit a motivation letter in English using the official template. The motivation letter should explain: Why the applicant wants to study in Slovakia Why the selected programme is appropriate How the applicant demonstrates talent What the applicant hopes to achieve academically and professionally Both the CV and motivation letter are described as essential application documents. How to Apply Applications must be submitted electronically through the official scholarship portal. Applications cannot be submitted by email. A complete application should include: Personal and citizenship information Education details Information about the chosen Slovak institution and programme SAT or SCIO results Certified identity documentation Required solemn declarations CV Motivation letter Supporting documents applicable to the applicant’s circumstances Applicants should not wait until the final day. The application may require certified documents, signatures, test certificates and information from a university, all of which may take time to organise. The active application announcement states that registration is open until 31 July 2026 . APPLY HERE Application Deadline The deadline for the Slovakia Scholarships for Talented International Students is: 31 July 2026 at 23:59 Central European Summer Time Applicants should confirm the time-zone difference in their country and submit well before the portal closes. Because the source material contains both an active application notice and older wording that registration had not yet opened, applicants should rely on the current online portal status and verify that the submission function is active before preparing a last-minute application. Tuition Fees The scholarship does not create an automatic universal tuition-fee waiver. Students admitted to a full-time foreign-language programme at a public Slovak higher education institution may have their tuition fees waived under the relevant Slovak legislation. However, the final decision belongs to the individual higher education institution. Applicants should therefore ask their chosen university: Whether tuition fees apply Whether scholarship recipients qualify for a waiver Whether the waiver covers the full tuition amount Whether any administrative or programme fees remain payable A scholarship applicant should not assume that receiving €500 per month also guarantees free tuition. Health Insurance Scholarship recipients may qualify for public health insurance in Slovakia under the applicable legislation where they: Do not have permanent residence in Slovakia Are not already insured in another European Union member state, European Economic Area contracting state or Switzerland Hold a scholarship under a programme approved by the Slovak Government Applicants should verify their individual insurance position before travelling, particularly because insurance arrangements may differ according to citizenship, residence status and existing coverage. Visa and Temporary Residence Requirements Non-EU students may need a national visa and temporary residence permit for study. Applicants currently outside Slovakia are advised to apply for a national Type D visa through the Slovak embassy or an authorised external service provider responsible for their country of residence. The published guidance recommends applying for a 120-day national D visa connected to a temporary-residence application for study. After arriving in Slovakia, students must apply for temporary residence for study within the permitted period. Students already legally present in Slovakia may be able to apply through a Foreign Police office. The scholarship does not remove the applicant’s responsibility to satisfy immigration, visa and residence requirements. Applicants should begin these procedures early, because university enrolment and scholarship eligibility may be affected by delays. Conditions for Keeping the Scholarship Successful applicants must remain enrolled in an eligible full-time programme. The scholarship may be lost where the student: Terminates the supported study programme Transfers to a different higher education institution Changes from full-time to part-time or external study Repeats a study year in circumstances not permitted by the programme Loses student status Interrupts studies beyond the permitted period Submits false or altered documents Fails to comply with inspections connected to the scholarship A student may be allowed to change to another eligible programme within the same institution if the studies remain continuous and the student does not lose their status, restart the first year or repeat an academic year. The scholarship cannot normally be transferred from one university to another. Important Application Advice Applicants should take the following steps before applying: Confirm that the chosen university is a public or state Slovak institution. Check that the selected programme appears on the eligible Smart Specialisation list. Apply separately for university admission. Confirm whether the programme is full-time and conducted in person. Verify that an eligible SAT or SCIO result is available. Prepare certified identity documents. Complete all required declarations by hand where instructed. Prepare the English CV and motivation letter. Confirm tuition-fee arrangements with the university. Research visa, temporary residence and health-insurance requirements. Submit the scholarship application before 31 July 2026. Keep copies of every document and submission confirmation. Applicants should also remember that reaching the minimum test score does not guarantee an award. Selection remains competitive because applicants are ranked and the number of available scholarships is limited. Conclusion The Slovakia Scholarships for Talented International Students 2026/27 provide a substantial opportunity for international students seeking to pursue higher education in the heart of Europe. With funding of €500 per month, eligible bachelor’s and combined-degree students may receive up to €15,000, while eligible master’s students may receive up to €10,000. The programme is available across selected academic fields and public or state higher education institutions in Slovakia. However, the application process is demanding. Applicants must satisfy citizenship and residence conditions, choose an eligible study programme, apply separately to a Slovak university, submit a valid SAT or SCIO result, prepare all required documents and complete the online application before the deadline. Interested students should begin by confirming programme eligibility and reviewing their standardised-test results. A complete application must be submitted by 31 July 2026 at 23:59 CEST . For more information about this opportunity, click here . Discover more global job opportunities, click here . title: One Young World Academy Introduces Planetary Leadership Course 2026: Free Online Learning Opportunity for Young Leaders Tackling Global Existential Threats
summary: As the world faces increasingly interconnected challenges such as climate change, artificial intelligence risks, global health emergencies, conflicts, and environmental degradation, there is a growing need for a new generation of leaders capable of addressing these complex issues through collaboration and long-term thinking. To help prepare young leaders for this responsibility, the One Young World Academy has launched an innovative online learning programme titled Introduction to Planetary Leadership . Developed with the sponsorship of the InterAction Council , this globally recognized e-learning course introduces participants to the concept of planetary leadership, explores humanity s most pressing existential threats, and presents Strategy X-EXIST , a comprehensive framework designed to safeguard the future of humanity. The programme offers participants expert-led instruction, practical leadership insights, and an internationally recognized digital certificate upon successful completion. It is specifically designed for professionals, community leaders, policymakers, entrepreneurs, NGO workers, and young changemakers who are passionate about creating sustainable solutions to global challenges. Whether you work in climate action, public health, peacebuilding, technology governance, education, public service, or community development, this free online course provides valuable knowledge and practical tools to help you become a more effective global leader. About the One Young World Academy The One Young World Academy is an internationally recognized digital learning platform that equips young leaders with knowledge, leadership skills, and practical solutions to solve some of the world s biggest challenges. Through expert-led courses, participants gain access to world-class educational content developed by internationally respected organizations and thought leaders. The Introduction to Planetary Leadership course expands this mission by helping learners understand how today s interconnected global crises require coordinated international leadership and innovative thinking. The programme emphasizes collaboration between governments, scientists, civil society organizations, businesses, communities, and young people to collectively build a safer and more sustainable future. What is Planetary Leadership? Planetary leadership is a leadership model that encourages people to think beyond national borders and individual sectors. Rather than solving problems independently, planetary leadership recognizes that global issues are interconnected and require cooperation among multiple stakeholders. The course demonstrates how young leaders can contribute to building resilient societies capable of responding to existential threats through collaboration, responsible governance, innovation, and long-term planning. Participants will learn why leadership in the modern world must focus not only on immediate challenges but also on protecting future generations. Understanding Strategy X-EXIST At the center of this course is Strategy X-EXIST , a comprehensive action plan developed by the InterAction Council. The strategy focuses on preventing and reducing existential risks through four interconnected pillars: 1. Planet This pillar addresses environmental sustainability and climate security. Participants explore topics including: Climate change Environmental degradation Biodiversity protection Ecological resilience Sustainable development Climate adaptation Environmental governance 2. People This section examines issues affecting human well-being, including: Global health Pandemic preparedness Human security Social resilience Public health systems Equity and inclusion 3. Peace Participants learn about: Conflict prevention Peacebuilding International cooperation Governance Security policy Diplomacy Community resilience 4. Prosperity The final pillar focuses on creating sustainable economies through: Responsible technology Ethical artificial intelligence Innovation Economic resilience Inclusive development Digital governance Why Planetary Leadership Matters Today s global challenges rarely exist in isolation. For example: Climate change affects food security. Food insecurity contributes to migration. Migration influences political stability. Political instability affects public health. Artificial intelligence impacts employment, governance, and cybersecurity simultaneously. The course teaches participants how these issues reinforce one another and why integrated solutions are essential. Instead of treating problems separately, learners discover systems thinking approaches that produce lasting change. What You Will Learn Participants will gain knowledge in several important areas, including: Understanding existential threats facing humanity Principles of planetary leadership Systems thinking Global governance Climate resilience Pandemic preparedness Responsible AI governance Peacebuilding strategies International cooperation Intergenerational leadership Community resilience Advocacy techniques Strategic leadership Risk reduction frameworks Expert-Led Learning Experience The programme brings together distinguished experts from the InterAction Council alongside internationally recognized specialists working on global security challenges. Participants will hear from: InterAction Council representatives Leading climate experts Public health professionals Peacebuilding specialists Artificial intelligence governance experts Young global leaders actively working on existential challenges One of the highlights is an introductory address by Dr. Jo Nurse , who discusses existential security threats and explains the importance of planetary leadership in today s world. The module also includes a keynote presentation explaining the complete Strategy X-EXIST framework. Course Structure The programme consists of one comprehensive learning module: Global and Planetary Security – Prevent and Reduce Risks from Existential Threats Approximate duration: 45 minutes The module includes: Video lectures Expert presentations Keynote sessions Action reading materials Practical leadership insights Real-world case studies Interactive learning resources Short assessment quiz The assessment helps participants evaluate their understanding of the course concepts before completing the programme. Topics Covered During the Course Throughout the module, learners explore: Existential security threats Global governance Planetary leadership Climate tipping points Pandemic preparedness Artificial intelligence governance Peacebuilding Systems thinking Advocacy strategies Leadership development Intergenerational collaboration Responsible innovation Community resilience Global cooperation Benefits of Joining the Programme Participants receive numerous valuable benefits, including: Learn from Global Experts Gain direct insights from internationally respected experts and policymakers working to address humanity s greatest challenges. Practical Leadership Skills Develop actionable strategies that can be implemented within your workplace, organization, community, or country. Internationally Recognized Certificate Upon successful completion, participants receive a verified digital certificate issued by the One Young World Academy. The certificate can be: Added to your CV Shared on LinkedIn Included in professional portfolios Used to demonstrate commitment to global leadership Guaranteed Access to an Additional MOOC Participants also receive guaranteed access to the Planetary Leadership Massive Open Online Course (MOOC) developed by the InterAction Council, allowing them to continue expanding their knowledge after completing the introductory programme. Expand Professional Knowledge Gain a deeper understanding of: Climate governance Global health AI regulation International cooperation Sustainable leadership Policy development Who Should Apply? The programme is intended for individuals who are already making positive contributions within their communities and organizations. Ideal applicants include: Young professionals Government officials Civil servants Community organizers NGO professionals Social entrepreneurs Business leaders Researchers Development practitioners Climate advocates Peacebuilders Technology professionals Public health workers Policymakers Youth leaders APPLY HERE Characteristics of Strong Applicants The course is particularly suitable for people who: Are actively creating positive change. Work on complex social or environmental challenges. Want to understand how global issues are interconnected. Value fairness, sustainability, and future generations. Prefer practical solutions over theoretical discussions. Have opportunities to influence positive change. Want to promote responsible technology. Care about ethical leadership. Seek sustainable leadership practices that prevent burnout. Why This Course Stands Out Unlike many leadership programmes that focus on individual sectors, this course adopts an interdisciplinary approach. Participants learn how climate, health, technology, peace, governance, and economic systems interact, allowing them to develop more effective and sustainable solutions. The programme also emphasizes long-term thinking, ethical leadership, and collaborative action, which are increasingly important in today s interconnected world. Visit HERE to learn more about the One Young World Academy Introduces Planetary Leadership Course 2026 Application Deadline Interested applicants should submit their registration before: Application Deadline: August 7 Applicants are encouraged to register as early as possible to secure their place. Conclusion The One Young World Academy – Introduction to Planetary Leadership offers an outstanding opportunity for emerging and experienced leaders to strengthen their understanding of today s most pressing global challenges. Through expert instruction, practical leadership tools, and the innovative Strategy X-EXIST framework, participants will gain valuable knowledge to help create resilient communities and contribute to a safer, more sustainable future. Whether your work focuses on climate action, public health, peacebuilding, artificial intelligence, community development, or public policy, this course provides practical insights that can strengthen your leadership and expand your impact. With a verified digital certificate, access to additional learning opportunities, and guidance from internationally recognized experts, this programme represents an excellent investment in your personal and professional development. Those interested should complete their registration before the August 7 deadline to take advantage of this unique global learning opportunity. title: United Nations Fund for Population Activities (UNFPA): APRO Technology-Facilitated Gender-Based Violence Partnership Call 2026
summary: Background of the UNFPA Asia-Pacific Regional Programme (APRO) UNFPA is launching a new Asia and Pacific Regional Programme covering the period from 2026 to 2029. The programme is aligned with the UNFPA Strategic Plan for the same period and the Programme of Action of the International Conference on Population and Development. The regional programme is intended to accelerate progress toward UNFPA’s three transformative results: Ending the unmet need for family planning Ending preventable maternal deaths Ending gender-based violence and harmful practices The programme also seeks to strengthen responses to demographic change while ensuring that no one is left behind. This includes promoting accessible, inclusive and rights-based programming for persons with disabilities and other populations facing exclusion. Its implementation covers the humanitarian-development-peace continuum, recognizing that sexual and reproductive health, gender equality and protection needs must be addressed in development settings, emergencies and fragile or conflict-affected environments. Why UNFPA Is Prioritizing Technology-Facilitated Gender-Based Violence Technological innovation and digitalization have created important opportunities to improve access to services, information, education and communication. However, digital platforms have also become environments in which violence against women and girls can be committed, intensified or extended. UNFPA’s approach recognizes an online-offline continuum. This means that violence occurring through digital technologies cannot always be separated from violence occurring in homes, workplaces, schools, communities or public spaces. Technology-facilitated abuse may affect survivors’ safety, privacy, dignity, access to services and participation in public life. Effective programming therefore requires more than isolated online-safety activities. It demands coordinated work involving survivor-centred services, legal and policy systems, digital security, technology design, evidence, ethical data management and prevention. UNFPA APRO is seeking partners that can contribute specialized knowledge and operational capacity to strengthen both the prevention of TFGBV and the response available to survivors across Asia and the Pacific. Purpose of the UNFPA Expression of Interest The purpose of the call is to identify qualified organizations that can collaborate with UNFPA APRO in delivering the Asia-Pacific Regional Programme. Selected partners may support activities involving: Specialized technical assistance Capacity-strengthening and professional training Survivor-centred support services Prevention and response programming Digital safety and security Legal and policy advice Research and evidence generation Monitoring and evaluation Technology-sector engagement Evidence-based advocacy Innovation and service delivery Eligible entities selected through the process will sign an Implementing Partnership Agreement with UNFPA APRO. This agreement will regulate the technical collaboration and partnership arrangement. Subject to the availability of funds, UNFPA APRO will subsequently work with successful implementing partners to agree on a work plan and enter into a funded collaboration. Selection through the Expression of Interest process therefore creates a potential pathway to funded implementation, but the announcement does not guarantee funding or state a predetermined grant amount. Organizations Invited to Submit an Expression of Interest UNFPA APRO is inviting Expressions of Interest from several categories of legally registered entities. Academic and research institutions Universities, research centres, think tanks and other academic institutions may apply where they possess relevant expertise in areas such as: Gender-based violence Digital safety Technology and society Public policy Human rights Law and justice Social research Monitoring and evaluation Statistical measurement Data systems Survivor-centred programming Academic applicants should demonstrate not only theoretical expertise but also their capacity to translate research into practical programming, policy advice, training or evidence-based advocacy. Civil society organizations Eligible civil society applicants include local and international NGOs, women-led organizations, youth-led organizations, civil society networks and rights-based organizations. Such organizations may be particularly relevant where they have direct experience working with: Survivors of gender-based violence Women and girls in diverse circumstances Young people Frontline responders Human rights defenders Women’s rights movements Youth rights movements High-risk civic actors Communities affected by humanitarian emergencies People facing digital exclusion or online threats Professional associations and regional technical bodies Professional associations and specialized regional institutions may submit an EOI where they can contribute technical knowledge, standards, training systems, practitioner networks or policy support. These may include associations or technical bodies working in social services, public health, law, justice, policing, digital technology, data governance, cybersecurity, research, evaluation or other relevant areas. Organizations of persons with disabilities Organizations of persons with disabilities are expressly included among eligible entities. Their participation can help ensure that TFGBV prevention and response systems are inclusive, accessible and designed around the rights and lived experiences of persons with disabilities. Specialized private-sector entities Private-sector entities may apply where appropriate, particularly when they possess specialized expertise in: Technology-facilitated gender-based violence Digital security Online safety Digital evidence Information management Safe technology development Ethical technology design Safety-by-design principles Private-sector participation must be relevant to the workstream proposed and consistent with UNFPA’s rights-based, ethical and survivor-centred requirements. Four Main TFGBV Workstreams Organizations may express interest in one or more of four specialized pillars. Pillar 1: Response The response pillar focuses on strengthening services, systems and professional capacity to assist survivors of technology-facilitated gender-based violence safely, ethically and confidentially. Training and capacity building Selected partners may help adapt and roll out UNFPA’s global TFGBV training package for frontline responders. Target participants may include professionals working in: Social work Health services Justice systems Police and law enforcement Local government Gender-based violence services Humanitarian response Training may be implemented across priority countries, including humanitarian contexts. Partners may also support the revision of national gender-based violence referral mechanisms so that they adequately incorporate survivor-centred responses to technology-facilitated abuse. This work is important because referral systems designed only for conventional forms of violence may not fully address digital evidence, online threats, data privacy, account security or the continuing relationship between online and offline harm. TFGBV responder support network UNFPA APRO intends to facilitate a regional support network for TFGBV responders. The network would allow digital programmers, gender-based violence specialists and frontline responders to exchange: Practical experience Technical guidance Response protocols Lessons from implementation Emerging risks Promising practices Referral approaches Survivor-safety considerations Qualified partners may be expected to help establish, coordinate or provide technical support to this network. Regional survivor-support mechanism The call includes the potential establishment of a regional mechanism providing safe, ethical and confidential services to survivors of technology-facilitated gender-based violence. Organizations interested in this area should be able to demonstrate strong safeguards relating to: Survivor confidentiality Informed consent Data protection Referral management Risk assessment Ethical case handling Staff training Accessibility Protection from retaliation or further harm Digital forensics Partners may be selected to develop or support digital-forensic protocols for TFGBV cases. These protocols should ensure that the collection and storage of digital evidence are: Safe Ethical Survivor-centred Confidential Technically reliable Consistent with relevant legal and protection requirements Digital evidence can be highly sensitive. Poor collection or storage practices may expose survivors to further harm, compromise confidentiality or weaken legal and institutional responses. Safe digital and information-management systems The response pillar also covers systems used by GBV service providers to manage digital information. Partners may support the design or implementation of secure systems that protect data held by organizations managing TFGBV cases. This may involve reinforcing survivor-centred information-management systems and helping service providers reduce risks such as unauthorized access, insecure storage, inappropriate information sharing or loss of sensitive case information. Pillar 2: Prevention The prevention pillar focuses on reducing the risk of TFGBV before harm occurs and strengthening the ability of vulnerable groups and frontline actors to operate safely in digital environments. Resilience of high-risk actors Selected partners may strengthen the digital security, safety and operational resilience of groups facing elevated online risks. These groups may include: Frontline responders Women’s rights movements Youth rights movements Human rights and civic actors Professionals managing sensitive survivor information Relevant interventions could include training, risk assessment, security protocols, technical guidance and institutional digital-safety measures. Diverting young people from harmful online subcultures The call also seeks support for developing digital-literacy modules and targeted programmes intended to divert young people away from the manosphere and other harmful online subcultures. This workstream requires organizations capable of developing prevention approaches that are: Evidence-based Age-appropriate Gender-responsive Culturally relevant Suitable for digital delivery Focused on long-term behavioural and social change Applicants proposing work under this area should clearly explain their experience with young people, online cultures, gender norms, digital literacy or violence-prevention programming. Pillar 3: Enabling Environment The enabling-environment pillar addresses the laws, policies, technologies, standards and financial systems that influence how TFGBV is prevented and addressed. Rights-based laws and policies Partners may help strengthen the ability of governments and civil society organizations to develop and implement laws and policies addressing technology-facilitated gender-based violence. Activities may include: Specialized legal advice Policy analysis Drafting support Peer learning Mentorship Institutional capacity development Rights-based assessment of existing legislation The focus should be on legal and policy approaches that protect survivors and promote rights without creating unintended harm, exclusion or excessive restrictions. Digital governance and technology-industry standards UNFPA APRO is also seeking expertise related to gender-responsive digital governance and technology-industry practices. Partners may advocate for or support the implementation of: Safety-by-design principles Ethical technology development Gender-responsive digital governance Improved technology-sector standards Safer digital products Platform policies addressing TFGBV Prevention measures built into technology before harm occurs Safety by design means considering user protection and foreseeable risks during the design and development of digital services rather than responding only after abuse has occurred. Sustainable financing Organizations may contribute evidence and investment cases that influence national planning and budgeting. The objective is to encourage governments to allocate dedicated resources for TFGBV prevention and response rather than treating technology-facilitated violence as an unfunded or temporary policy issue. This work may involve: Costing interventions Developing financing cases Budget analysis Public-finance advocacy Evidence-based investment proposals Engagement with national planning institutions Applicants interested in this component should be capable of connecting technical TFGBV programming with national planning, public budgets and sustainable financing mechanisms. Pillar 4: Research, Data and Evaluation The fourth pillar focuses on building the evidence, measurement systems and ethical monitoring frameworks needed to improve TFGBV programmes and policies. Ethical monitoring and evaluation Partners may support the development of monitoring and evaluation frameworks for TFGBV programmes. These frameworks should contain appropriate: Indicators Means of verification Data-collection methods Progress measures Outcome measures Impact-assessment approaches Ethical safeguards Monitoring systems must be survivor-centred and should not create additional risks for survivors or expose confidential information. The resulting evidence should help UNFPA and its partners assess implementation, understand impact and improve future programming and policy. Evidence generation and knowledge sharing Selected entities may generate, synthesize and share evidence concerning TFGBV prevention and response at: Regional level Subregional level National level This may include: Trend analysis Literature reviews Comparative research Programme evaluations Policy research Documentation of promising practices Evidence briefs Knowledge products Regional learning initiatives Statistical standards and prevalence measurement The call includes support for developing statistical standards and testing methods for measuring the prevalence of technology-facilitated gender-based violence. Organizations interested in this work should demonstrate expertise in areas such as survey design, gender statistics, research ethics, measurement, methodology development or sensitive-data collection. Administrative-data systems Partners may also help improve existing administrative-data systems by integrating variables that allow reported TFGBV cases to be identified and tracked. This work must balance the need for accurate information with survivor safety, confidentiality, informed consent and responsible data governance. Selection Criteria UNFPA APRO will assess applicants against several technical and institutional requirements. Legal registration Applicants must be legally registered entities. Both not-for-profit organizations and, where appropriate, for-profit organizations may be considered. Applicants should be prepared to provide official registration documents and evidence of their legal status. Technical capacity The organization must demonstrate proven experience and expertise in at least one of the four TFGBV pillars. A strong submission should clearly explain: The workstream or workstreams selected Relevant previous projects Technical methodologies used Results achieved Populations or institutions supported Geographic coverage Relevant publications, tools or training materials The organization’s comparative advantage Institutional capacity Applicants must demonstrate the institutional ability to implement activities effectively. UNFPA expects evidence of: Established internal governance Clear operational policies Human-resource policies Adequate staffing Results-based management capacity Robust monitoring and evaluation systems Suitable implementation and reporting arrangements Applicants should avoid making unsupported statements about institutional strength. Claims should be backed by policies, organizational structures, previous performance or concrete examples. Financial capacity Organizations must have experience managing funds and a sound financial-management system. The required financial capacity includes: Robust internal controls Fraud-prevention policies Established accounting procedures Reliable financial reporting Evidence of responsible fund management The organization’s financial systems must be supported by recent unqualified audit reports, such as audit reports covering the last two years. An unqualified audit opinion generally means that the external auditor concluded that the financial statements were presented fairly in all material respects under the relevant reporting framework. Applicants whose latest audit reports contain qualifications should carefully assess whether they meet this criterion before applying. Ethical standards Potential partners must demonstrate their commitment to UNFPA policies and ethical standards. Particular emphasis is placed on UNFPA’s zero-tolerance approach to sexual exploitation, abuse and harassment, commonly addressed through Prevention of Sexual Exploitation and Abuse requirements. Applicants should be able to demonstrate appropriate safeguarding policies, reporting mechanisms, staff conduct requirements and accountability procedures. Geographical relevance The applicant must have a demonstrated track record of successfully managing and implementing programmes in Asia and the Pacific. The announcement does not expressly require every applicant to be headquartered within the region. However, organizations must provide credible evidence of relevant implementation experience, partnerships, networks or operational capacity in Asia and the Pacific. Required Expression of Interest Documents Interested organizations must prepare an Expression of Interest containing the following elements. 1. Cover letter The cover letter should identify: The organization submitting the EOI The selected area or areas of interest The relevant TFGBV pillar or pillars How the organization aligns with the UNFPA Asia-Pacific Regional Programme Why the organization is interested in collaborating with UNFPA APRO The organization’s principal comparative advantage The cover letter should be direct, evidence-based and tailored to the specific call. 2. Evidence of technical expertise Applicants must provide evidence of their technical capacity and record of similar work. Relevant evidence may include: Previous project descriptions Completion reports Evaluation findings Research publications Training materials Policies or technical tools developed Contracts or partnership agreements Examples of survivor-centred services Digital-safety programmes Advocacy outcomes Relevant organizational profiles Experience working with United Nations agencies is considered an advantage, although the announcement does not state that previous UN experience is mandatory. 3. Evidence of partnerships and networks Applicants must explain their partnerships, networks and comparative advantage within Asia and the Pacific. This may include relationships with: Government institutions Civil society networks Women-led organizations Youth-led organizations Research institutions Technology-sector actors Survivor-service providers Organizations of persons with disabilities Regional bodies International development partners The submission should explain how these networks would help the applicant deliver regional, subregional or national-level work. 4. Proposed modalities of engagement Applicants must indicate how they propose to collaborate with UNFPA APRO. Possible modalities identified in the call include: Technical support Research Advocacy Innovation Service delivery Organizations may propose more than one modality where this is consistent with their capacity and the selected workstream. A strong proposal should explain the proposed approach, expected contribution, institutional role and how the organization would coordinate with UNFPA and other partners. How to Submit the UNFPA APRO Expression of Interest Applications must be submitted by email. Submission email: apro.office@unfpa.org Required subject line: EOI on TFGBV Application deadline: 14 August 2026 Applicants should ensure that the correct subject line is used so the submission can be properly identified and routed. Before sending the email, organizations should verify that: The cover letter is signed by an authorized representative. The selected TFGBV workstream is clearly identified. All supporting evidence is attached. The organization’s legal name and contact information are accurate. Attached files open correctly. File names are clear and professionally formatted. The email is sent to the correct address. A copy of the complete submission is retained. Delivery confirmation or an email record is preserved. The official announcement does not specify a required application portal or closing time. Applicants should therefore follow the stated email-submission process and send their EOI before the deadline date. Funding and Partnership Arrangements The call does not publish a fixed grant amount, budget ceiling or guaranteed level of financial support. Eligible entities selected through the process will enter into an Implementing Partnership Agreement with UNFPA APRO. Subject to funding availability, UNFPA APRO will then coordinate with successful implementing partners to agree on a work plan and establish a funded collaboration. Organizations should therefore understand the process as involving two stages: Identification of technically and institutionally qualified partners through the Expression of Interest. Development of an agreed work plan and funded collaboration, subject to available resources. Submission of an EOI does not itself guarantee selection, funding or a specific financial award. Practical Tips for Preparing a Strong Submission Select only relevant workstreams Applicants should not claim expertise across all four pillars unless they can provide substantial evidence. A focused application demonstrating deep experience in one or two areas may be more credible than a broad application containing unsupported claims. Connect experience directly to TFGBV General experience in gender equality, gender-based violence or information technology may not be sufficient on its own. Applicants should clearly demonstrate how their experience relates to the interaction between digital technology and gender-based violence. Demonstrate survivor-centred practice Organizations proposing response, data, forensic or information-management activities should explain how they protect: Safety Privacy Confidentiality Dignity Informed consent Accessibility Survivor decision-making Provide evidence, not promotional language Statements such as “we are a leading organization” or “we have extensive experience” should be supported by: Project dates Countries covered Funding managed Outputs delivered People or institutions reached Evaluations completed Policies influenced Systems established Review financial documentation early Because recent unqualified audit reports are specifically mentioned, applicants should confirm the availability and status of their latest external audits before preparing the complete EOI. Explain regional value Applicants should show how their proposed contribution would be relevant beyond a single isolated activity. This may include regional networks, transferable tools, multilingual capacity, cross-country experience, policy connections or the ability to adapt interventions for different Asia-Pacific contexts. Submit before 14 August 2026 The official notice does not specify a closing hour. Applicants should not wait until the final day, especially where internal approvals or large supporting documents are involved. APPLY HERE. Conclusion The UNFPA APRO Expression of Interest presents a significant institutional collaboration opportunity for organizations working at the intersection of gender-based violence, technology, digital safety, survivor services, public policy, research, data and human rights. Through the call, UNFPA seeks partners capable of contributing to four major areas: survivor-centred response, prevention, an enabling legal and technological environment, and research, data and evaluation. Eligible organizations must demonstrate legal registration, relevant technical expertise, institutional governance, sound financial systems, appropriate ethical standards and a record of programme implementation in Asia and the Pacific. Successful entities may enter into an Implementing Partnership Agreement with UNFPA APRO and subsequently develop a funded work plan, subject to the availability of resources. Organizations considering an application should identify the workstream that best matches their expertise, assemble strong technical and financial evidence, explain their regional comparative advantage and submit the complete Expression of Interest to apro.office@unfpa.org using the subject line “EOI on TFGBV” no later than 14 August 2026 For more global opportunities, click HERE. title: Google DeepMind AI Master’s Scholarships 2026–2027 at GATE Institute, Sofia University (Fully Funded): Apply for Up to USD 28,695 to Study Artificial Intelligence in Bulgaria
summary: Artificial intelligence is rapidly transforming industries, economies, and societies across the world. As the demand for highly skilled AI professionals continues to grow, access to quality postgraduate education has become increasingly important for aspiring researchers, engineers, data scientists, and technology innovators. To help nurture the next generation of AI experts, the GATE Institute at Sofia University “St. Kliment Ohridski” has officially opened applications for the Google DeepMind AI Master’s Scholarships 2026–2027 . This prestigious scholarship programme offers two fully funded scholarships for outstanding students pursuing Master s studies in Artificial Intelligence and related disciplines at the Faculty of Mathematics and Informatics (FMI), Sofia University . The initiative is funded through Google DeepMind , one of the world s leading artificial intelligence research organizations, with the objective of increasing access to advanced AI education while supporting talented students from diverse backgrounds who demonstrate academic excellence and financial need. Unlike previous years, where only one scholarship was available, the 2026–2027 academic year introduces two fully funded scholarships , doubling the opportunity for deserving students to pursue graduate education in one of Europe s leading AI research environments. Applicants selected for this highly competitive programme will receive comprehensive financial support throughout their studies, enabling them to concentrate on academic excellence, research, innovation, and professional development without the burden of financial constraints. About the Google DeepMind AI Master’s Scholarships The Google DeepMind AI Master’s Scholarships are designed to encourage talented individuals to pursue advanced education in artificial intelligence and computer science while promoting greater diversity and inclusion within the technology sector. Since its launch in 2017 , Google DeepMind has partnered with universities worldwide to provide scholarship opportunities that support students with exceptional academic potential who may face financial barriers to postgraduate education. The scholarship programme has expanded significantly over the years and has supported scholars at universities across numerous countries, including: Bulgaria Colombia Greece Poland Romania South Africa Turkey United Kingdom United States Canada France The programme aims to strengthen the global AI ecosystem by empowering talented students with access to world-class education, research opportunities, and professional networks. Study at Sofia University s Faculty of Mathematics and Informatics Successful applicants will undertake their Master s studies at the Faculty of Mathematics and Informatics (FMI) , one of Bulgaria s leading institutions for mathematics, computer science, artificial intelligence, and information technologies. Students admitted through the scholarship programme will become part of an academic environment recognized for excellence in research, innovation, and interdisciplinary collaboration. Eligible Master s programmes include: Artificial Intelligence Information Retrieval and Knowledge Discovery Big Data Technologies Each of these programmes prepares students with advanced theoretical knowledge and practical skills needed to address complex technological challenges in academia, industry, and research. Scholarship Benefits Each scholarship provides financial support throughout the entire 18-month duration of the eligible Master s programme. The indicative total funding available to each successful scholar is: USD 28,695.06 The scholarship package covers numerous educational and personal expenses, ensuring scholars can dedicate their full attention to their studies. Financial support includes: Full tuition fees for the Master s programme Housing expenses Living costs Academic travel support Equipment expenses Relocation expenses Access to AI-related academic conferences This comprehensive funding package enables students to participate fully in academic activities, international conferences, research collaborations, and professional development opportunities without significant financial concerns. Why This Scholarship Matters Artificial intelligence continues to reshape sectors such as: Healthcare Finance Agriculture Manufacturing Transportation Cybersecurity Climate research Education Public administration The Google DeepMind AI Master s Scholarships aim to prepare graduates capable of contributing innovative AI solutions to these and many other sectors. The increase from one scholarship to two scholarships demonstrates the growing commitment of Google DeepMind and GATE Institute to developing future AI specialists and making postgraduate education more accessible. Eligibility Requirements Applicants must satisfy all scholarship eligibility requirements before applying. To qualify, candidates must: Be ordinarily resident in Bulgaria or another European Union (EU) Member State. Achieve a candidate-student examination success rate of at least 4.50 . Hold a Bachelor s degree with a minimum overall grade of 4.50 . Apply for and be admitted to one of the eligible Master s programmes at Sofia University for the 2026–2027 academic year. Meet the scholarship programme s financial eligibility criteria. Financial Eligibility Requirements Financial need forms an important component of the selection process. Eligibility will be determined based on: Total household income. Number of family members living within the household. The calculated income per household member must fall below the minimum salary level in Bulgaria as of 1 January 2026 . Applicants should prepare supporting financial documentation to demonstrate compliance with these requirements. Required Application Documents Applicants must submit a complete application package before the deadline. Required documents include: Completed scholarship application form Updated Curriculum Vitae (CV) Motivation letter Bachelor s degree diploma and/or academic transcripts demonstrating academic performance Financial eligibility documents Proof of application or admission to one of the eligible Master s programmes Additional supporting documents where applicable Only complete applications submitted before the deadline will be considered. Selection Process Applications will be assessed by an independent selection panel comprising: Professors from the Faculty of Mathematics and Informatics, Sofia University Representatives of GATE Institute The evaluation process will include: 1. Eligibility Verification Applicants will first be assessed to ensure they satisfy all scholarship eligibility requirements. 2. Academic Assessment The panel will evaluate: Academic achievements Degree performance Examination results Educational background 3. Motivation and Qualifications The committee will carefully examine: Motivation letter Curriculum Vitae Relevant experience Professional qualifications Career aspirations Potential contribution to the AI field 4. Financial Assessment Financial documentation will be reviewed to determine whether applicants satisfy the programme s financial eligibility requirements. Following the evaluation process: Two scholarship recipients will be selected. One additional candidate will be placed on a waiting list. The final nomination of scholars will be completed by 31 October 2026 . Important Dates Applicants should carefully note the following timeline: Activity Date Applications Open 07 July 2026 Application Deadline 09 October 2026 Admission examinations/admission According to the official Faculty of Mathematics and Informatics website Final Selection of Scholars By 31 October 2026 Academic Year Begins October 2026 Applicants are encouraged to begin preparing their documentation early to avoid last-minute delays. About GATE Institute The GATE Institute is Bulgaria s first Centre of Excellence dedicated to Big Data and Artificial Intelligence . The institute brings together: Researchers Students Industry experts Government institutions International partners Its mission is to develop cutting-edge technologies and innovative data-driven solutions addressing important societal and economic challenges. Students studying within the GATE research environment benefit from: Interdisciplinary collaboration AI research projects Big Data technologies Machine learning applications Innovation ecosystems International partnerships Exposure to real-world technological challenges This research-rich environment provides excellent preparation for careers in academia, research institutions, technology companies, startups, and international organizations. Visit HERE to know more about the Google DeepMind AI Master’s Scholarships 2026–2027 at GATE Institute, Sofia University Why You Should Apply The Google DeepMind AI Master s Scholarships provide far more than financial assistance. Successful scholars will gain: Access to one of Europe s emerging AI research centres. Full financial support throughout their Master s studies. Opportunities to attend AI conferences. Exposure to interdisciplinary research. Professional networking opportunities. Hands-on experience with cutting-edge AI technologies. Preparation for careers in research and industry. For students passionate about artificial intelligence, machine learning, data science, and related technologies, this represents an exceptional opportunity to earn a postgraduate degree while minimizing financial barriers. How to Apply Interested applicants should complete the official scholarship application form before the deadline. Application Deadline: 09 October 2026 Applicants should ensure all required supporting documents are included before submission. APPLY HERE For additional enquiries: career@gate-ai.eu Conclusion The Google DeepMind AI Master s Scholarships 2026–2027 at GATE Institute and Sofia University offer one of the most attractive fully funded postgraduate opportunities available in Europe for students pursuing careers in artificial intelligence. By expanding the programme from one scholarship to two, Google DeepMind and GATE Institute are strengthening their commitment to supporting talented students and advancing innovation in AI. With funding worth approximately USD 28,695.06 , coverage of tuition fees, living expenses, housing, academic travel, equipment, relocation costs, and conference participation, recipients will have the financial freedom to focus entirely on their education and research. Students who meet the academic and financial eligibility requirements are strongly encouraged to apply before 09 October 2026 and take advantage of this outstanding opportunity to study in a dynamic research environment dedicated to shaping the future of artificial intelligence. title: Academic Internship Opportunity – Swiss Mission to the UN in Rome, Fall 2026
summary: The Permanent Mission of Switzerland to the United Nations Organisations in Rome is offering a full-time academic internship (100%) beginning November 1, 2026 , for a duration of 12 months . This highly competitive program is designed for Swiss graduates who want to gain practical experience in diplomacy, food security, and sustainable development while representing Switzerland’s interests at the UN agencies headquartered in Rome. About the Mission The Mission represents Switzerland’s positions in food and agriculture at the three Rome-based UN agencies: FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization) – global food security and agricultural development. IFAD (International Fund for Agricultural Development) – rural development and poverty reduction. WFP (World Food Programme) – hunger relief and emergency food assistance. Together, these agencies form the backbone of international food governance. The Swiss Mission in Rome works closely with them to advance Switzerland’s priorities in food systems transformation , financing for development , and the governance of the Committee on World Food Security (CFS) . Switzerland also maintains four other UN Missions in Geneva, New York, Paris, and Vienna. Collectively, these missions contribute to Switzerland’s active engagement in solving global challenges related to peace, human rights, poverty reduction, food security, and environmental protection. The Mission in Rome is particularly strategic because food and agriculture are central to global stability. Hunger and malnutrition are not only humanitarian issues but also drivers of migration, conflict, and inequality. By participating in this internship, candidates will be directly involved in Switzerland’s efforts to strengthen international cooperation, promote sustainable food systems, and ensure that global governance structures remain inclusive and effective. Internship Responsibilities Interns will support the Mission’s activities by: Assisting with Switzerland’s positions in FAO, IFAD, and WFP governing bodies. Contributing to policy analysis and documentation for the CFS . Monitoring developments in food systems transformation and financing for development. Drafting briefs, reports, and speeches for Swiss representatives. Networking with diplomats and officials from other missions and UN agencies. Supporting events and meetings hosted by the Mission. This internship offers a comprehensive learning experience in international diplomacy , policy analysis , and multilateral governance . In practice, this means interns will attend high-level meetings, take notes, prepare summaries, and ensure that Switzerland’s positions are clearly articulated. They may also be asked to conduct background research on emerging issues such as climate change impacts on agriculture, innovative financing mechanisms for food security, or the role of technology in transforming food systems. This exposure provides a rare opportunity to see how diplomacy and technical expertise intersect in shaping international policy. Eligibility Requirements Applicants must meet the following criteria: Education – University degree, preferably Master’s, in natural sciences, food/agricultural sciences, international relations, political science, or economics. Citizenship – Must be a Swiss citizen. Languages – Excellent command of English and one Swiss official language; good knowledge of another Swiss official language. Skills – Interest in diplomacy, strong communication, analytical ability, flexibility, and initiative. Graduation Timeline – Applications accepted within 12 months of graduation. The emphasis on language skills reflects Switzerland’s multilingual identity. Interns who can communicate fluently in German, French, Italian, and English will be particularly well-positioned to engage with diverse stakeholders. Analytical ability is equally critical, as interns must interpret complex policy documents and distill them into concise recommendations. Internship Details Start Date: November 1, 2026 Duration: 12 months (full-time, 100%) Location: Via Aventina 32, Rome, Italy Reference: Internship MiRo, 11/2026 Rome itself offers a vibrant backdrop for this internship. As the headquarters of FAO, IFAD, and WFP, the city is a hub for international discussions on food and agriculture. Interns will not only work within the Mission but also have opportunities to attend conferences, workshops, and cultural events that enrich their professional and personal experience. Required Documents (all in one PDF file) Motivation letter in a Swiss national language. CV with photo. Copies of diplomas. Work and internship certificates (if available). Important Dates Deadline: August 2, 2026, early applications are highly encouraged. Written Tests: August 26, 2026 Interviews: September 3, 2026 Applicants should ensure that their motivation letter highlights not only academic achievements but also personal commitment to Switzerland’s values of neutrality, dialogue, and humanitarian engagement. The written test will likely assess analytical and writing skills, while the interview will evaluate communication ability, motivation, and cultural fit. Application Process Applications must be submitted by email to: roma.onu.candidature@eda.admin.ch to the attention of: Permanent Mission of Switzerland to the United Nations organisations in Rome, Via Aventina 32, 00153, Rome Phone: +39 06 89 58 151 roma.onu@eda.admin.ch FAQs 1. Is this internship paid? It is an academic internship at 100% workload. Details on compensation are typically provided during the selection process. 2. Who can apply? Only Swiss citizens who graduated within the past 12 months are eligible. 3. What languages are required? Excellent English and one Swiss official language; knowledge of another Swiss official language is an advantage. 4. What is the duration of the internship? 12 months, starting November 1, 2026. 5. What kind of work will I do? Policy analysis, drafting reports, supporting Switzerland’s positions in FAO, IFAD, WFP, and contributing to food systems transformation discussions. 6. Do I need prior diplomatic experience? Not required, but interest in diplomacy, analytical skills, and initiative are essential. 7. How competitive is the selection? Highly competitive. Written tests and interviews ensure only the most qualified candidates are selected. 8. Can I apply if I studied outside Switzerland? Yes, as long as you are a Swiss citizen and meet the academic requirements. 9. What is the application deadline? August 2, 2026. 10. Where will the internship take place? At the Permanent Mission of Switzerland to the UN Organisations in Rome, Via Aventina 32. 11. Will I gain international exposure? Yes, interns will interact with diplomats, UN officials, and representatives from other missions, gaining valuable international experience. 12. What skills will I develop? Analytical writing, policy research, diplomatic communication, and networking in a multilateral environment. 13. Do interns participate in official UN events? Yes, interns may attend sessions of FAO, IFAD, WFP, and CFS, providing them with direct exposure to multilateral negotiations. 14. What career paths can this internship lead to? Many former interns pursue careers in international organizations, Swiss federal administration, NGOs, or academia. The skills gained are highly transferable. For further details about the Swiss Mission to the UN in Rome, Fall 2026 Academic Internship Opportunity, click HERE . Conclusion The Academic Internship at the Swiss Mission to the UN in Rome offers Swiss graduates a unique opportunity to engage in international diplomacy and food governance. Interns will gain valuable experience in multilateral negotiations, policy analysis, and global cooperation, while contributing to Switzerland’s mission of promoting peace, food security, and sustainable development. This internship is more than a professional stepping stone; it is a chance to contribute to Switzerland’s global mission of fostering dialogue, cooperation, and sustainable solutions. For motivated graduates, it represents a career-defining experience that combines academic knowledge with real-world diplomacy. To learn more about other internship opportunities, visit the OFY page HERE .
